book_claims.claim: Low-protein, high-complex-carbohydrate diet produces maximum longevity and health in mice
book_claims.mechanism: Does not activate IGF-1/TOR-S6K/PKA pro-aging pathways as strongly as protein or simple sugars
book_claims.study_ref: Solon-Biet SM et al., Cell Metabolism 2014 (ch4-8)

book_claims.claim: Whole grains recommended as a complex carbohydrate source; part of Sardinian and Molochio centenarian diets
book_claims.mechanism: High fiber slows glucose absorption; provides B vitamins, minerals, and sustained energy

book_claims.claim: Complex carbohydrates support healthy weight management comparable to low-carb diets without muscle or water loss
book_claims.mechanism: Adequate carbs preserve glycogen and muscle protein; low-carb diets cause loss of water and lean mass
book_claims.study_ref: Yang MU, Van Itallie TB, Journal of Clinical Investigation 1976 (ch4-21)

book_claims.claim: Maximizing complex carbohydrates from whole grains while minimizing sugar is recommended for diabetes prevention
book_claims.mechanism: Complex carbohydrates cause a slower, lower insulin response than simple sugars and refined starches

supplementary_research.finding: Millet consumption is associated with a significantly lower risk of type 2 diabetes development in meta-analysis; finger millet lowered postprandial blood glucose more than rice or wheat (PMID 31690332)

Millets are naturally gluten-free and high in resistant starch and phenolic antioxidants; their regular consumption in traditional African and South Asian longevity populations is well documented (PMID 25614185)

Millet provides 6 g protein, 2.3 g fiber, and notable amounts of magnesium and phosphorus per 100 g cooked; it is gluten-free and has a moderate glycemic index, making it a suitable longevity grain for those with gluten sensitivity

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Moderate bioavailability; phytate binding reduced by soaking; supports insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular health
Important for bone matrix and cellular energy (ATP); co-ingestion with vitamin D improves utilization
Resistant starch acts as prebiotic; fermented by gut bacteria into short-chain fatty acids
NAD+ precursor; supports mitochondrial function and DNA repair

synergies.description: Millet's methionine complements the lysine-rich profile of legumes, together forming a complete amino acid profile — a staple pairing across African and Indian longevity diets
Monounsaturated fats from olive oil slow glucose absorption from millet starch and aid fat-soluble vitamin uptake
Vitamin C significantly enhances absorption of millet's non-heme iron by converting Fe³⁺ to the more bioavailable Fe²⁺ form
